title.en: Online Seller Not Refunding Your Money? Complaint Ladder from Platform to Consumer Court

answerBox.en: When an online seller or marketplace refuses a due refund: exhaust the platform's grievance officer (the E-Commerce Rules give them 48 hours to acknowledge, one month to resolve), call the National Consumer Helpline 1915, then file on e-Daakhil — free for claims up to ₹5 lakh, from your own city. Card/UPI chargebacks through your bank run in parallel.

en.title: Build the refund record
en.summary: Order, payment, defect/non-delivery proof, and the policy.
en.detail: Save the order confirmation, payment proof, delivery status, photos/video of the defective or wrong item (an **unboxing video** is gold), chat/call records, and — critically — a screenshot of the **refund/return policy as it stood at purchase**. Under the **Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules, 2020**, sellers cannot refuse to take back defective/deficient goods or refuse refunds where amounts were charged for undelivered services.

en.title: Force the platform's grievance machinery
en.summary: Named grievance officers with statutory clocks exist — use them.
en.detail: Every marketplace must publish a **grievance officer** who acknowledges within **48 hours** and resolves within **one month**. Write formally (not just app tickets), cite the order and rule, and demand the refund with a deadline. Escalate simultaneously to the **National Consumer Helpline — 1915 / [consumerhelpline.gov.in](https://consumerhelpline.gov.in)** — its convergence process gets responses from partnered companies within weeks, free.

en.title: Chargeback: the parallel money lever
en.summary: Your bank can reverse card/UPI payments for non-delivery.
en.detail: Dispute the transaction with your card issuer/bank citing **goods not delivered / not as described** — card-network chargeback rules give real reversal rights within tight windows (typically 60–120 days from transaction), and RBI's UPI dispute framework covers failed/fraudulent debits. Attach your grievance trail. A successful chargeback often ends the matter before any court step.

en.title: e-Daakhil from your own city
en.summary: No fee up to ₹5 lakh; platform + seller both as parties.
en.detail: If the refund still hasn't come: a **₹999 advocate's notice** to the platform and seller (registered offices), then file on **[e-Daakhil](https://edaakhil.nic.in)** naming **both the marketplace and the seller** — filing where *you* reside or work. Claim the refund with interest, compensation for harassment, and costs. See the full process in our [consumer forum guide](/help/consumer-complaints/consumer-forum-complaint-guide); small-value cases are genuinely viable at zero court fee.

rows.label: e-Daakhil filing
rows.note: Slab fees above; no lawyer mandatory

en.footnote: COD orders lack the chargeback lever — prefer prepaid via credit card for expensive online purchases precisely because the dispute rights are stronger.

en: online seller refund nahi de raha

en.q: The platform says "seller's responsibility, not ours". Is that right?
en.a: No. The E-Commerce Rules make marketplaces responsible for grievance redressal and for not allowing unfair practices on their platform, and consumer commissions routinely hold platform and seller jointly liable. Name both — the platform has the deeper pocket and the compliance pressure.

en.q: How long do I have to file the consumer complaint?
en.a: Two years from the cause of action (the refusal/deficiency). But act in days, not months: platform clocks (48 hours/1 month), chargeback windows (60–120 days) and evidence freshness all favour early movers.

en.q: My order was "delivered" but never arrived. Who bears that?
en.a: The seller/platform must prove delivery to you — a courier scan is not gospel. Dispute in writing immediately, demand the POD (proof of delivery with signature/OTP), file the chargeback for non-delivery, and escalate. Commissions treat fake-delivery cases as deficiency plus unfair trade practice.

en.q: The seller offers only a coupon/wallet credit instead of a refund. Must I accept?
en.a: No — where a refund is due (defective goods, non-delivery, cancellation per policy), you can insist on money to the original payment source. Wallet-credit-only terms for such cases sit poorly with the rules and are regularly overridden in commission orders.

Yes — the ladder up to e-Daakhil costs nothing but time: grievance officer, 1915, chargeback are all free, and filing itself has no fee at this value. Sellers know small claimants rarely persist; the ones who do usually get paid at the notice or helpline stage.

reviewer: Adv. Sneha Iyer, Bar Council of Tamil Nadu & Puducherry, TN/1187/2013
author: WakilBhai Editorial Team
